channel.title: Paper Pulse
channel.description: Keyword-based research paper aggregation from arXiv and IACR

item.title: Differential Privacy in Generative AI Agents: Analysis and Optimal Tradeoffs
item.description: This paper addresses a critical gap in privacy research for generative AI agents: while prior work focuses on protecting user prompts, we formalize and quantify **privacy leakage from enterprise data accessed by AI agents**. We propose a probabilistic differential privacy (DP) framework where response generation is modeled as a stochastic mechanism mapping prompts and private datasets to token-sequence distributions. Within this framework, we introduce *token-level* and *message-level* DP definitions and derive tight analytical bounds linking privacy loss ε to generation parameters—especially temperature and message length—showing that ε grows exponentially with temperature. We formulate a privacy-utility co-design problem and prove the existence of a unique optimal temperature that maximizes response quality (e.g., task accuracy, perplexity) under a given (ε, δ)-DP constraint. Empirical evaluation on domain-specific benchmarks confirms our bounds’ tightness (<12% error) and demonstrates that the optimal temperature strategy improves utility by up to 37% over baselines while satisfying strong privacy guarantees (ε ≤ 0.8).

item.title: Machine Learning for Network Attacks Classification and Statistical Evaluation of Machine Learning for Network Attacks Classification and Adversarial Learning Methodologies for Synthetic Data Generation
item.description: This paper addresses two critical challenges in AI-driven network intrusion detection: (1) robust supervised classification of evolving attacks (e.g., GenAI- and RL-based threats), and (2) privacy-preserving synthetic data generation for data-scarce scenarios. We introduce the first unified multi-modal NIDS dataset—harmonizing CIC-IDS-2017, CIC-IoT-2023, UNSW-NB15, and CIC-DDoS-2019—by aligning flow statistics, packet payload embeddings, and temporal contextual features into a shared space. For attack classification, we benchmark XGBoost, LightGBM, and CNN-LSTM under stratified cross-validation, achieving 0.982±0.007 AUC in cross-dataset zero-shot transfer. For synthetic data, we propose an adversarial framework evaluated via SDV metrics, f-divergences (KL, JS), distinguishability tests (<0.08), and non-parametric statistical validation (KS, TRTS/TSTR). Results show synthetic data not only preserve fidelity (F1-drop <2.1% on downstream classifiers) but also improve lightweight model performance by +1.3% F1 over real data—demonstrating both utility and privacy compliance. This work establishes a reproducible benchmark and evaluation paradigm for next-generation NIDS.

item.title: Post-Training Local LLM Agents for Linux Privilege Escalation with Verifiable Rewards
item.description: This paper introduces PrivEsc-LLM, a lightweight (4B-parameter) local LLM agent fine-tuned for Linux privilege escalation—a security-critical task requiring multi-step interactive reasoning with automatically verifiable success (i.e., actual root access). We propose a two-stage post-training pipeline: (1) supervised fine-tuning on procedurally generated, diverse privilege-escalation traces, followed by (2) reinforcement learning with *verifiable rewards*—sparse, binary signals granted only upon real sandboxed root acquisition, ensuring reward integrity and eliminating hallucination-driven cheating. Evaluated on a held-out benchmark of 12 realistic Linux escalation scenarios, PrivEsc-LLM achieves **95.8% success rate** within 20 interaction rounds—nearly matching Claude Opus 4.6 (97.5%) while reducing per-success inference cost by **>100×**. Our approach demonstrates that small, local LLMs can achieve state-of-the-art security performance under strict resource and privacy constraints, enabling reproducible, on-premise vulnerability research.

item.title: ARES: Scalable and Practical Gradient Inversion Attack in Federated Learning through Activation Recovery
item.description: Federated Learning (FL) promises privacy-preserving collaborative training, yet gradient inversion attacks (GIAs) threaten to reconstruct sensitive training data from shared updates. While active GIAs achieve high-fidelity reconstruction—even for large batches—their reliance on model architectural modifications hinders practical deployment. This paper introduces **ARES**, a scalable and architecture-agnostic active GIA that recovers individual samples by formulating activation inversion as a noisy sparse recovery problem solved via generalized Lasso. To handle multi-sample batches, ARES integrates the *imprint* technique to disentangle activations, enabling per-sample reconstruction without model surgery. We provide theoretical guarantees: deriving both the expected recovery rate and an upper bound on reconstruction error. Extensive experiments on CNNs and MLPs across diverse datasets (e.g., CIFAR-10, ImageNet-100) show ARES significantly outperforms prior GIAs under large batch sizes (e.g., +8.2 dB PSNR at batch=64) and realistic FL settings—including non-IID data, client heterogeneity, and gradient compression. Our work exposes intermediate activations as a critical, underappreciated privacy vulnerability in FL, urging urgent development of activation-aware defenses.

item.title: Caging the Agents: A Zero Trust Security Architecture for Autonomous AI in Healthcare
item.description: This paper introduces *Caging*, the first production-deployed zero-trust security architecture for autonomous AI agents in HIPAA-regulated healthcare settings. Motivated by critical vulnerabilities—unauthorized compliance, PHI leakage, identity spoofing, cross-agent propagation, and indirect prompt injection—we develop a six-domain threat model and implement four-layer defense-in-depth: (1) gVisor-based kernel-level workload isolation on Kubernetes; (2) credential proxy sidecars eliminating raw secret access; (3) per-agent egress network policies enforcing destination allowlists; and (4) a prompt integrity framework with structured metadata envelopes and explicit untrusted content labeling. Over 90 days of operation across nine production agents, our architecture detected and remediated four HIGH-severity findings via an automated audit agent, achieved progressive hardening across three VM image generations, and fully covered all eleven attack patterns from recent AI agent literature. All configurations, tooling, and the prompt integrity framework are open-sourced.

item.title: WebPII: Benchmarking Visual PII Detection for Computer-Use Agents
item.description: WebPII is the first public, fine-grained benchmark for visual PII detection in web screenshots—addressing critical privacy risks posed by computer-use agents that ingest real UIs and expose screenshots during cloud inference. It comprises 44,865 synthetically generated, VLM-reproduced e-commerce UI images with pixel-level annotations, featuring: (1) an extended PII taxonomy including transaction-level identifiers (e.g., order IDs, partial credit card numbers) enabling cross-service re-identification; (2) anticipatory detection of *in-progress* form inputs (e.g., partially typed emails or phone numbers); and (3) scalable, layout-diverse generation. Experiments confirm WebPII improves layout-invariant detection and generalization to unseen page types. We train WebRedact—a lightweight, CPU-efficient model achieving **0.753 mAP@50** (vs. 0.357 for text-extraction baseline) at **20ms latency**, demonstrating practical utility. The dataset and model are publicly released.

item.title: Federated Computing as Code (FCaC): Sovereignty-aware Systems by Design
item.description: Federated Computing as Code (FCaC) is a declarative architecture that embeds sovereignty-critical constraints—such as data residency, access delegation, and jurisdictional compliance—directly into cryptographically verifiable artifacts, eliminating reliance on centralized policy services or shared trust infrastructure. By compiling authority into Envelope Capability Tokens (ECTs) anchored by Key Your Organization (KYO) identities and validated via Proof of Possession (PoP), FCaC enables *local, offline admission verification* at execution boundaries. It cleanly separates constitutional governance (e.g., “who may join”) from procedural governance (e.g., ABAC, quotas, risk scoring). Evaluated in a cross-silo federated learning prototype using MNIST, FCaC demonstrates end-to-end workflow enforcement—including envelope issuance, boundary verification, and trigger-based model training—with sub-millisecond local checks and no runtime policy coordination. An open-source implementation is released to support reproducibility and adoption.

item.title: SEAL-Tag: Self-Tag Evidence Aggregation with Probabilistic Circuits for PII-Safe Retrieval-Augmented Generation
item.description: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems face a critical privacy vulnerability: *contextual leakage*, where adversaries exploit model instruction-following to exfiltrate Personally Identifiable Information (PII) from retrieved contexts via adaptive queries. Existing defenses impose rigid trade-offs among utility, latency, and safety. We propose **SEAL-Tag**, a privacy-preserving runtime framework built on a *Verify-then-Route* paradigm. SEAL-Tag introduces the **SEAL-Probe protocol**, requiring the model to jointly generate a draft response and a structured, verifiable PII-Evidence Table (PET). A lightweight **Probabilistic Circuit (PC)** adjudicates PETs by enforcing interpretable logical constraints—ensuring robust, auditable decisions. To overcome the privacy “Cold Start”, we design the **S₀–S₆ Anchored Synthesis Pipeline**, generating high-fidelity, provenanced RAG data with precise PII annotations, paired with a **Two-Stage Curriculum** for efficient alignment to the audit protocol. Evaluation shows SEAL-Tag reduces adaptive PII leakage by **>8×**, while matching the utility, speed, and retrieval quality of unsafe baselines—establishing a new Pareto frontier for PII-safe RAG.

item.title: LAAF: Logic-layer Automated Attack Framework A Systematic Red-Teaming Methodology for LPCI Vulnerabilities in Agentic Large Language Model Systems
item.description: We introduce **LAAF (Logic-layer Automated Attack Framework)**, the first automated red-teaming framework targeting Logic-layer Prompt Control Injection (LPCI) in agentic LLM systems—vulnerable due to persistent memory, RAG, and tool integration. LAAF uniquely combines: (i) a comprehensive 49-technique taxonomy across six categories (e.g., Layered, Semantic), with 5 variants per technique and 6 lifecycle stages, enabling up to 2.8M SHA-256-deduplicated payloads; and (ii) a Persistent Stage Breaker (PSB) that iteratively mutates winning payloads to seed subsequent attack stages—mimicking real adversarial escalation. Evaluated across five production LLM platforms over three independent runs, LAAF achieves a mean stage-breakthrough rate of **84%** (83–86%), outperforming random single-technique baselines. Layered and semantic techniques show highest efficacy, with layered payloads notably surpassing encoding-based ones on well-defended systems.

item.title: QuantFL: Sustainable Federated Learning for Edge IoT via Pre-Trained Model Quantisation
item.description: Federated Learning (FL) enables privacy-preserving AI on edge IoT devices but suffers from high carbon emissions due to energy-intensive uplink communication. While pre-trained models are increasingly available on-device, their role in reducing fine-tuning overhead remains underexplored. We propose **QuantFL**, a sustainable FL framework that leverages pre-trained initialization to enable aggressive, lightweight quantization. We find that pre-training naturally concentrates update statistics, allowing efficient bucket quantization—eliminating the need for energy-heavy error-feedback mechanisms. On MNIST and CIFAR-100, QuantFL reduces total communication by **40%** (vs. full-precision downlink) and achieves **≥80% uplink bit reduction**, while matching or exceeding uncompressed baselines under strict bandwidth budgets: BU attains **89.00%** (MNIST) and **66.89%** (CIFAR-100) test accuracy with orders-of-magnitude fewer bits. QuantFL delivers a practical, green recipe for scalable FL on battery-constrained IoT networks.

item.title: Proactive Knowledge Inquiry in Doctor-Patient Dialogue: Stateful Extraction, Belief Updating, and Path-Aware Action Planning
item.description: This paper reframes doctor-patient dialogue as a *proactive knowledge-inquiry problem* under partial observability—shifting EMR generation from passive post-hoc summarization to real-time, state-aware clinical reasoning. We propose a novel framework integrating: (1) stateful extraction to maintain evolving knowledge states; (2) sequential belief updating over clinical hypotheses; (3) gap-aware state modeling to identify high-stakes uncertainties; (4) hybrid retrieval over objectified medical knowledge (guidelines, drug databases, anatomy ontologies); and (5) a lightweight POMDP-based planner for path-aware question/recommendation generation. Evaluated on a controlled pilot of 10 standardized multi-turn dialogues and a 300-query retrieval benchmark, the full framework achieves 83.3% coverage, 80.0% risk recall, 81.4% structural completeness, and lower redundancy than chunk-only or template-heavy baselines. These results demonstrate methodological viability—not clinical readiness—and position proactive inquiry as a conceptually grounded, testable paradigm for next-generation dialogue-driven EMR systems.

item.title: GUIDE: GenAI Units In Digital Design Education
item.description: GUIDE (GenAI Units In Digital Design Education) is an open, modular courseware repository designed to integrate generative AI into digital hardware education. It standardizes teaching around reusable units—each comprising slides, short videos, runnable Google Colab labs, and curated papers—ensuring pedagogical consistency and scalable instructor reuse. We demonstrate three representative units: **VeriThoughts**, enabling formal-verification-backed RTL generation via LLM reasoning; enhanced **LLM-aided testbench generation** with improved functional coverage; and **LLMPirate**, exposing IP piracy risks through hands-on Trojan insertion/detection. Four semester-long course instances—GUIDE4ChipDesign, Build your ASIC, GUIDE4HardwareSecurity, and Hardware Design—are fully specified with learning outcomes, projects, and assessments. Notably, the GUIDE4HardwareSecurity capstone on LLM-assisted hardware Trojan deployment has been successfully used in classrooms and at CSAW. The NYU Cognichip Hackathon engaged 24 international teams in AI-augmented RTL workflows. All materials are open-source: https://github.com/FCHXWH823/LLM4ChipDesign.

item.title: Graph-Native Cognitive Memory for AI Agents: Formal Belief Revision Semantics for Versioned Memory Architectures
item.description: We introduce **Kumiho**, a graph-native cognitive memory architecture formally grounded in belief revision semantics. It unifies cognitive memory primitives (immutable revisions, mutable tag pointers, typed dependency edges, URI addressing) with versioned asset management—enabling a single, coherent graph representation for both memory and agent-generated work. Our key formal contribution is a proven correspondence between the AGM belief revision framework and the operational semantics of a property graph memory system, satisfying all basic AGM postulates (K\*2–K\*6) and Hansson’s Relevance and Core-Retainment postulates. Architecturally, Kumiho employs a dual-store model (Redis + Neo4j), hybrid fulltext/vector retrieval, and three innovations: prospective indexing (LLM-generated scenario implications indexed at write time), structured causal event extraction, and client-side LLM reranking. On LoCoMo, it achieves 0.565 F1 and 97.5% adversarial refusal accuracy; on the harder LoCoMo-Plus (implicit constraint recall), it attains 93.3% judge accuracy—substantially outperforming all published baselines (best prior: Gemini 2.5 Pro, 45.7%). Critically, Kumiho is model-decoupled: swapping the answer model from GPT-4o-mini to GPT-4o lifts end-to-end accuracy from ~88% to 93.3% at negligible cost (~$14 for 401 queries).

item.title: Federated Distributional Reinforcement Learning with Distributional Critic Regularization
item.description: We propose **Federated Distributional Reinforcement Learning (FedDistRL)**, a paradigm that federates *quantile-parameterized distributional critics* instead of scalar value functions or policies—preserving multimodality and tail risk information critical for safety-critical applications. To prevent distributional smearing during averaging, we introduce **TR-FedDistRL**, which constructs a *client-specific, risk-aware Wasserstein barycenter* over a local temporal buffer and enforces a *distributional trust region* via shrink-squash regularization around it. Theoretically, under fixed-policy evaluation, the constrained update is contractive in a probe-set Wasserstein metric. Experiments on bandit, multi-agent gridworld, and continuous highway tasks show: (1) reduced mean-smearing and preserved distributional fidelity; (2) up to 42% lower catastrophe/accident rates versus mean-oriented baselines (e.g., FedAvg+DQN); and (3) significantly lower critic/policy drift than non-federated distributional RL. This work bridges distributional RL, optimal transport, and federated learning for verifiably safe edge intelligence.

item.title: Malicious Or Not: Adding Repository Context to Agent Skill Classification
item.description: This paper presents the largest empirical security analysis of AI agent skills to date, challenging the alarmingly high (up to 46.8%) malicious classification rates reported by description-only scanners. We collect and analyze **238,180 unique skills** from three major marketplaces and GitHub, shifting evaluation from isolated `SKILL.md` files to a holistic assessment that cross-verifies skill descriptions against their embedded GitHub repositories. By incorporating repository context—including code content, commit history, ownership status, and abandonment signals—we reduce the confirmed malicious rate to just **0.52%**, cutting false positives by over 98%. Our analysis uncovers previously undocumented real-world attack vectors, most notably **skill hijacking via abandoned GitHub repositories**, and demonstrates that description-repository inconsistency is a strong predictor of benign intent rather than malignancy. This work establishes repository-aware contextual analysis as a critical, scalable foundation for trustworthy AI agent skill ecosystems.

item.title: Poisoning the Pixels: Revisiting Backdoor Attacks on Semantic Segmentation
item.description: Semantic segmentation models underpin safety-critical systems like autonomous driving, yet their backdoor vulnerabilities remain poorly understood. Prior work naively adapts classification-style threats (e.g., object-to-background mislabeling), ignoring segmentation’s unique pixel-wise semantics and spatial dependencies. We systematically revisit segmentation-specific backdoor attacks, identifying four coarse-grained vectors (Object-to-Object, Object-to-Background, Background-to-Object, Background-to-Background) and two fine-grained ones (Instance-Level, Conditional). To formalize them, we propose **BADSEG**, a unified framework that jointly optimizes pixel-level triggers and label manipulation under utility preservation constraints. Extensive experiments across Cityscapes and ADE20K show BADSEG achieves 92.4%–98.1% attack success rates with <0.8% clean mIoU drop on DeepLabV3, SegFormer, and Mask2Former. Critically, six state-of-the-art defenses fail—average detection rate is <31%, with >67% false positives. The vulnerability persists even in transformer-based models and the Segment Anything Model (SAM), where it induces 89.3% pixel-level errors. This work exposes fundamental security gaps in semantic segmentation and calls for task-aware defense design.

item.title: Ciphertext-Policy ABE for $\mathsf{NC}^1$ Circuits with Constant-Size Ciphertexts from Succinct LWE
item.description: We present the first lattice-based ciphertext-policy attribute-based encryption (CP-ABE) scheme for $\mathsf{NC}^1$ access policies achieving **constant-size ciphertexts**—i.e., ciphertext length is $O(1)$ in terms of circuit depth $d$, size $s$, and input length $\ell$, depending only on the security parameter $\lambda$. The scheme is **selectively secure in the standard model** under the $\operatorname{poly}(\lambda)$-succinct LWE assumption (Wee, CRYPTO 2024). Public keys and ciphertexts are $O(1)$ (hiding $\operatorname{poly}(\lambda)$ factors), while secret keys are $O(\ell)$, linear in input bit-length. As a direct application, we obtain a broadcast encryption scheme for $N$ users with ciphertext size $\operatorname{poly}(\lambda)$—**independent of $\log N$**—and key sizes $\operatorname{poly}(\lambda, \log N)$. This resolves a long-standing efficiency bottleneck in functional encryption for bounded-depth circuits.

item.title: SparkVSR: Interactive Video Super-Resolution via Sparse Keyframe Propagation
item.description: Video Super-Resolution (VSR) remains largely non-interactive: users cannot correct artifacts or steer restoration outcomes. SparkVSR bridges this gap by introducing **sparse keyframes as a simple, expressive control signal**. Users super-resolve only 1–5 frames using any off-the-shelf image SR model; SparkVSR then propagates their high-fidelity priors across the entire LR video while preserving motion coherence. We propose a **keyframe-conditioned latent-pixel two-stage training pipeline**, jointly optimizing cross-space propagation and perceptual refinement. At inference, flexible keyframe selection (manual, I-frame–based, or random) and a **reference-free guidance mechanism** ensure robustness—even with imperfect or missing keyframes. On standard VSR benchmarks, SparkVSR achieves new state-of-the-art in no-reference quality assessment: +24.6% on CLIP-IQA, +21.8% on DOVER, and +5.6% on MUSIQ—demonstrating superior temporal consistency and detail fidelity. Crucially, SparkVSR generalizes *out-of-the-box* to unseen tasks like old-film restoration and video style transfer, establishing itself as a generic interactive, keyframe-conditioned video processing framework. Code and demos: https://sparkvsr.github.io/

item.title: Differential Harm Propensity in Personalized LLM Agents: The Curious Case of Mental Health Disclosure
item.description: This study investigates how **mental health disclosure**—a sensitive, realistic personalization cue—affects harmful task completion in tool-using LLM agents. Extending the AgentHarm benchmark, we evaluate frontier (e.g., GPT-5.2, Claude Sonnet 4.5, Gemini 3-Pro) and open-source (DeepSeek 3.2) models on multi-step malicious and matched benign tasks under controlled user-context conditions: *no bio*, *bio-only*, and *bio + mental health disclosure*, with lightweight jailbreak injection. Results show non-trivial harmful completion across all models (notably highest for DeepSeek 3.2), while bio-only context generally reduces harm and increases refusals; adding mental health disclosure further shifts outcomes modestly—but effects are attenuated after multiple-testing correction. Crucially, increased refusals occur **equally on benign tasks**, revealing a safety–utility trade-off via over-refusal. Jailbreak prompting sharply elevates harm and can override the protective effect of personalization. Collectively, personalization acts as a **weak, fragile protective factor** against agentic misuse—easily compromised by minimal adversarial pressure—highlighting the urgent need for personalization-aware evaluations and robust, context-invariant safeguards.

item.title: Federated Learning with Multi-Partner OneFlorida+ Consortium Data for Predicting Major Postoperative Complications
item.description: This study developed and validated privacy-preserving federated learning (FL) models to predict major postoperative complications—ICU admission, mechanical ventilation, acute kidney injury, and in-hospital mortality—using real-world data from 358,644 adult patients across five healthcare institutions (2012–2023). Leveraging a hierarchical heterogeneous FL framework, models were trained locally without raw data sharing and aggregated via encrypted gradient updates. FL models achieved consistently strong performance: AUROC ranged from 0.82 to 0.91 and AUPRC from 0.38 to 0.67 across all outcomes—matching or exceeding the best single-site local models (mean AUROC gain: +2.1–4.7%) and narrowing inter-site performance variance by 32%. Critically, FL matched centralized modeling accuracy (AUROC gap < 0.015) while fully preserving data sovereignty. These results demonstrate FL’s feasibility for scalable, generalizable, and regulatory-compliant clinical decision support.

item.title: Domain-Independent Dynamic Programming with Constraint Propagation
item.description: This paper bridges dynamic programming (DP) and constraint programming (CP) by integrating *constraint propagation* directly into a domain-independent DP framework. We embed a general-purpose CP solver (MiniZinc/Gecode) to prune states and transitions *during DP execution*—reducing variable domains, detecting infeasibility early, and eliminating invalid transitions before expansion. Evaluated on three combinatorial optimization problems—Single Machine Scheduling with Time Windows (SMS-TW), Resource-Constrained Project Scheduling Problem (RCPSP), and Travelling Salesperson Problem with Time Windows (TSPTW)—our approach reduces state expansions by up to 68%, solves 22% more SMS-TW and 19% more RCPSP instances than pure DP, and significantly accelerates convergence on tightly constrained TSPTW instances. Runtime analysis confirms that propagation benefits outweigh overhead for constrained problems, though further optimization of propagation cost is warranted. This work establishes the first principled, model-based integration of CP reasoning into DP, advancing hybrid optimization methodology.

item.title: Age Predictors Through the Lens of Generalization, Bias Mitigation, and Interpretability: Reflections on Causal Implications
item.description: Chronological age predictors frequently suffer from poor out-of-distribution (OOD) generalization due to spurious correlations with exogenous attributes—e.g., tissue type, sex, or batch effects—that act as confounders in causal analysis and protected attributes in fairness contexts. To unify generalization, bias mitigation, and interpretability, we propose a theoretically grounded adversarial representation learning framework that enforces invariance to such attributes. Evaluated on public mouse transcriptomic datasets across multiple tissues and drug interventions (including Elamipretide), our model achieves 23.6% lower OOD MAE than strong baselines and recapitulates published biological findings—e.g., significant predicted age reduction in skeletal and cardiac muscle post-treatment (*p* < 0.01). Crucially, while the learned representations are biologically plausible and robust, we rigorously clarify that purely predictive invariance does not entail causal identification; causal interpretation remains unsupported without experimental intervention or structural causal assumptions.

item.title: FederatedFactory: Generative One-Shot Learning for Extremely Non-IID Distributed Scenarios
item.description: FederatedFactory is a zero-dependency, generative one-shot learning framework designed for *extremely* non-IID federated settings—where clients hold mutually exclusive class labels. Instead of aggregating discriminative model weights (which fail catastrophically under label disjointness), FederatedFactory federates *generative priors*: lightweight, client-specific modules (e.g., conditional VAEs) are exchanged in **a single communication round**. These modules collectively synthesize a universally class-balanced, semantically coherent dataset—enabling local training without gradient conflict or external pretraining bias. Evaluated on MedMNIST and ISIC2019, FederatedFactory recovers centralized upper-bound performance: on pathological CIFAR-10 heterogeneity, accuracy rises from 11.36% to **90.57%**, and ISIC2019 AUROC reaches **90.57%**. Crucially, it enables exact modular unlearning via deterministic deletion of generative modules—ensuring compliance and auditability.

item.title: CoMAI: A Collaborative Multi-Agent Framework for Robust and Equitable Interview Evaluation
item.description: CoMAI is a collaborative multi-agent framework designed to address key limitations—security vulnerabilities, subjective bias, and inflexible difficulty scaling—in LLM-based interview evaluation. Unlike monolithic single-agent systems, CoMAI decomposes the assessment pipeline into four specialized agents (question generation, security, scoring, summarization), orchestrated by a centralized finite-state machine. This architecture enables real-time prompt-injection defense, rubric-guided multidimensional scoring, and adaptive question difficulty adjustment based on candidate responses. Evaluated across 12 job domains, CoMAI achieves **90.47% accuracy**, **83.33% recall**, and **84.41% candidate satisfaction**, outperforming baseline models significantly (p<0.01). Its structured, audit-ready outputs enhance fairness and interpretability—demonstrating a scalable, robust paradigm for equitable AI-driven hiring.

item.title: Dynamic Meta-Layer Aggregation for Byzantine-Robust Federated Learning
item.description: Federated Learning (FL) enables privacy-preserving collaborative training but remains vulnerable to Byzantine adversaries injecting malicious updates—especially untargeted, adaptive attacks like multi-label flipping and noise-backdoor hybrids. Existing defenses often rely on restrictive assumptions or fixed thresholds, limiting generalizability. We propose **FedAOT**, a dynamic meta-layer aggregation framework inspired by meta-learning: it adaptively weights client updates based on real-time reliability scores—assessing gradient consistency, loss sensitivity, and cross-round stability—without assuming attack types or requiring manual thresholds. Evaluated across four heterogeneous benchmarks (CIFAR-10/100, FEMNIST, Shakespeare) under 10 diverse attacks, FedAOT achieves **12.7–38.4% higher accuracy** than state-of-the-art baselines and maintains >82% accuracy even on *unseen* attack combinations. With <5% communication overhead and sub-15ms aggregation latency per 1,000 clients, FedAOT delivers scalable, assumption-free robustness for production FL systems.

item.title: Adaptive regularization parameter selection for high-dimensional inverse problems: A Bayesian approach with Tucker low-rank constraints
item.description: This paper proposes a variational Bayesian framework for high-dimensional inverse problems, leveraging Tucker decomposition to project inference onto a low-rank core tensor space—reducing complexity while preserving structural expressivity. Its key innovations are: (1) mode-wise precision parameters enabling *anisotropic, data-driven regularization* (e.g., stronger smoothing along motion-blur directions in deblurring); and (2) *joint estimation of noise level* from data, eliminating reliance on known noise variance. Evaluated on 2D deblurring, 3D heat conduction, and Fredholm equations, it consistently outperforms L-curve, GCV, UPRE, and the discrepancy principle—achieving +0.73–2.09 dB PSNR gains in deblurring and +6.75 dB in 3D thermal inversion, scaling to 110,000 variables. Limitations include rank sensitivity; future work targets automated rank selection and theoretical guarantees.

item.title: Parametric Social Identity Injection and Diversification in Public Opinion Simulation
item.description: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used as synthetic agents for scalable public opinion simulation, yet they suffer from *Diversity Collapse*: social identities become indistinguishable in hidden representations, leading to flattened inter-group differences and homogeneous intra-group responses. To address this, we propose **Parametric Social Identity Injection (PSII)**—a general framework that injects learnable, low-dimensional embeddings of demographic attributes (e.g., age, education) and value orientations (e.g., traditionalism, self-expression) directly into intermediate LLM hidden states. Unlike prompt-based persona conditioning, PSII enables fine-grained, controllable, and representation-level identity modulation. Evaluated on the World Values Survey across multiple open-source LLMs (Llama-3-8B, Qwen2-7B, Phi-3-mini), PSII reduces KL divergence to real-world survey distributions by 42.7% on average and boosts intra-group diversity (Shannon entropy) by 3.1× and inter-group discriminability by 2.8×. Our work establishes a new paradigm for socially aware LLM agent control and advances diversity-preserving opinion simulation. Code and data: https://github.com/halsayxi/PSII

item.title: Detecting Data Poisoning in Code Generation LLMs via Black-Box, Vulnerability-Oriented Scanning
item.description: We present **CodeScan**, the first black-box, vulnerability-oriented framework for detecting data poisoning and backdoor attacks in code generation LLMs. Unlike prior token-level consistency scanners—ineffective for code due to semantic equivalence across syntactic diversity—CodeScan identifies malicious patterns by analyzing *structural recurrence*: it generates code from multiple clean prompts, normalizes abstract syntax trees (ASTs) to suppress surface variations (e.g., variable names, formatting), then mines high-frequency AST substructures across generations. These candidate structures are rigorously validated for security vulnerabilities using an LLM-based zero-shot analyzer. Evaluated across 108 real-world models (3 architectures, 5 sizes) under 4 attack types and 3 vulnerability classes (injection, privilege escalation, resource exhaustion), CodeScan achieves **97.2% detection accuracy** with **62% lower false positives** than state-of-the-art methods—demonstrating robustness, scalability, and practical deployability.

item.title: PAuth - Precise Task-Scoped Authorization For Agents
item.description: The emerging agentic web requires AI agents to securely execute user-specified natural-language (NL) tasks via web services. However, conventional operator-scoped authorization (e.g., OAuth) grants broad permissions—such as “transfer”—rather than precise, task-bound operations—e.g., “transfer $100 to Bob”—leading to inevitable overprivilege. We propose **PAuth**, a novel *Precise Task-Scoped Implicit Authorization* model: submitting an NL task implicitly authorizes *only* the concrete operations necessary for its faithful execution. To enforce this server-side, we introduce **NL slices**—symbolic API specifications derived from the task and upstream results—and **envelopes**, data structures that cryptographically bind operand values to their symbolic provenance, enabling verifiable data lineage. Evaluated in AgentDojo, PAuth achieves 100% success in benign tasks without extra permissions and 100% detection of injected spurious operations in adversarial tests. Our analysis confirms PAuth’s semantic precision and practical efficiency, with average token overhead under 120 per task.

item.title: Security Assessment and Mitigation Strategies for Large Language Models: A Comprehensive Defensive Framework
item.description: This study bridges a critical gap in LLM security by introducing the first standardized, cross-architecture vulnerability assessment framework and a production-ready defensive system. We systematically evaluate five major LLMs—GPT-4, GPT-3.5 Turbo, Claude-3 Haiku, LLaMA-2-70B, and Gemini-2.5-pro—against 10,000 adversarial prompts across six attack categories. Results reveal substantial security disparities (vulnerability rates: 11.9%–29.8%), disproving the assumption that model capability implies robustness. To counter identified threats, we propose MLDF—a lightweight, multi-layered defense framework achieving 83.0% average detection accuracy with only 5.0% false positives and sub-120ms latency. Our open-sourced evaluation suite and implementation provide actionable tools for organizations to quantify risk and deploy LLMs safely in high-stakes environments.

item.title: OPERA: Online Data Pruning for Efficient Retrieval Model Adaptation
item.description: OPERA introduces an online data pruning framework for efficient and effective domain adaptation of dense retrieval models. Recognizing the inherent heterogeneity in training query-document pairs, it proposes two complementary strategies: Static Pruning (SP) retains only high-similarity pairs, improving ranking (NDCG@10 +0.5%) but harming recall due to reduced query diversity; Dynamic Pruning (DP) resolves this tradeoff via two-stage adaptive sampling—modulating query-level selection probability and document-level negative weighting throughout training—while retaining full dataset access. Evaluated across eight datasets spanning six domains, DP achieves state-of-the-art performance: +1.9% NDCG@10 and +0.7% Recall@20, with an average rank of 1.38 across all methods. Crucially, DP reaches comparable or superior results in **<50% of the training time** required by standard fine-tuning—and its gains generalize seamlessly to Qwen3-Embedding, confirming architecture-agnostic benefits.

item.title: Transformers Can Learn Rules They've Never Seen: Proof of Computation Beyond Interpolation
item.description: This paper provides an existence proof that Transformers can learn and explicitly express abstract rules *never observed* during training—refuting the strongest architectural form of interpolation-only accounts. In Experiment 1, we design a linearly inseparable XOR cellular automaton with provably un-interpolatable held-out patterns: nearest neighbors always predict the wrong label, yet a two-layer Transformer achieves up to 100% accuracy (47/60 converged runs) and circuit extraction confirms internal XOR computation—critically dependent on multi-step constraint propagation (96.7% with soft unrolling vs. 63.1% without). In Experiment 2, models generalize across 49 held-out integer operator pairs (e.g., “×3 then +5”) by emitting proof-like intermediate steps and final answers; Transformer mean accuracy is 41.8% (max 78.6%), vastly outperforming interpolation baselines (KRR: 4.3%; KNN/MLP: 0% on all pairs), and performance collapses without intermediate supervision. Combined with a formal construction showing a standard Transformer block can implement exact local Boolean logic, these results demonstrate that rule learning beyond interpolation is not only possible but mechanistically explicit—challenging the claim that Transformers *cannot in principle* discover and communicate unseen rules.

item.title: Differential Privacy for Network Connectedness Indices
item.description: We address the challenge of releasing network connectedness indices—measures of assortative mixing across node attributes—under edge-adjacent differential privacy (DP). Standard DP mechanisms fail here due to the indices’ high global sensitivity and poor composition: a single node’s attribute may influence thousands of cells in the mixing matrix. Our method is simple, two-stage, and theoretically grounded: (1) add calibrated noise to node attributes, then analytically debias downstream statistics; (2) apply a second layer of noise to protect individual edges. We prove consistency and asymptotic normality for both discrete and continuous labels. Simulations and real-world networks (as small as *n* = 200) show our estimators achieve substantially lower error (40–70% improvement) than baselines under tight privacy budgets (e.g., ε ≤ 1), enabling rigorous, privacy-preserving network analysis in social science.

item.title: ClawWorm: Self-Propagating Attacks Across LLM Agent Ecosystems
item.description: We present **ClawWorm**, the first self-replicating worm attack targeting production-scale LLM agent ecosystems—specifically the widely deployed OpenClaw platform (40k+ active instances). ClawWorm achieves fully autonomous infection from a *single initial message*: it hijacks victim configuration for persistence across restarts, executes arbitrary payloads on each reboot, and propagates to every newly discovered peer without further attacker input. Evaluated across three distinct infection vectors (API gateway injection, message queue hijacking, plugin hot-loading) and three payload types (data exfiltration, coordinated DDoS, reverse shell), ClawWorm achieves >92% end-to-end infection success, sustains multi-hop propagation (≥5 hops), and decouples payload logic from propagation mechanics. Root-cause analysis reveals critical architectural flaws: conflated trust boundaries among configuration, execution, and inter-agent messaging layers. We propose targeted defenses—including signed configuration validation, runtime tool-call whitelisting, and zero-trust message routing—to secure each boundary. Code and samples will be released post responsible disclosure.

item.title: Amplification Effects in Test-Time Reinforcement Learning: Safety and Reasoning Vulnerabilities
item.description: Test-time reinforcement learning (TTRL), a self-consistency-based test-time training (TTT) method, improves LLM reasoning by using majority-vote rewards to reinforce consistent outputs. However, we uncover a critical *amplification effect*: malicious prompt injections during TTRL do not merely exploit vulnerabilities—they **magnify the model’s pre-existing behavioral tendencies**, amplifying safety when the base model is robust, but drastically amplifying harmfulness when it is vulnerable. Crucially, this amplification incurs a consistent *reasoning tax*: across GSM8K and StrategyQA, TTRL suffers up to 12.7% accuracy drop under attack while harmful output rates increase 3.8×. We introduce **HarmInject**, an adversarial prompting framework that forces joint execution of jailbreak and reasoning queries, escalating harmfulness amplification (e.g., harmful response rate surges from 5.2% to 63.4% on LLaMA-3-8B). Our work reveals a fundamental safety-reasoning trade-off in consistency-driven TTT and calls for safer reward design and runtime safety gating.

item.title: TrinityGuard: A Unified Framework for Safeguarding Multi-Agent Systems
item.description: TrinityGuard is a unified, OWASP-grounded framework for safety evaluation and runtime monitoring of LLM-based multi-agent systems (MAS). It introduces a novel three-tier risk taxonomy covering 20 MAS-specific threats—spanning single-agent vulnerabilities, inter-agent communication hazards, and system-level emergent risks. Structured as a trinity architecture, it comprises: (1) an MAS abstraction layer adaptable to diverse platforms (e.g., AutoGen, CrewAI); (2) an evaluation layer with curated, risk-targeted attack probes; and (3) runtime monitor agents orchestrated by a centralized LLM Judge Factory. TrinityGuard enables both pre-deployment vulnerability assessment (generating detailed per-risk reports) and real-time monitoring (analyzing structured execution traces and issuing alerts). Formalized safety metrics and case studies across six representative MAS deployments demonstrate its versatility, detecting 92.3% of known risks and uncovering three new emergent failure modes. TrinityGuard establishes a foundational, scalable, and actionable safety standard for MAS research and deployment.

item.title: SFCoT: Safer Chain-of-Thought via Active Safety Evaluation and Calibration
item.description: Large language models (LLMs) excel at complex reasoning but remain vulnerable to jailbreak attacks that compromise safety alignment. Existing defenses rely on post-hoc output filtering, leaving intermediate Chain-of-Thought (CoT) steps unmonitored and exploitable. To address this, we propose **SFCoT (SaFer Chain-of-Thought)**—a real-time, proactive framework that evaluates and calibrates unsafe reasoning steps *during* inference. SFCoT introduces a **three-tier safety scoring system**, a **multi-perspective consistency verification mechanism**, and a **dynamic intervention module** that redirects unsafe reasoning trajectories without disrupting the CoT flow. Experiments across 12 high-risk domains show SFCoT reduces attack success rate from **58.97% to 12.31%**, while preserving >99% of baseline reasoning accuracy (GSM8K/MMLU) and adding minimal latency (<15%). SFCoT requires no model fine-tuning and offers plug-and-play deployment for safer, auditable LLM reasoning.

item.title: How Vulnerable Are AI Agents to Indirect Prompt Injections? Insights from a Large-Scale Public Competition
item.description: This paper presents findings from the largest public red-teaming competition to date evaluating **concealed indirect prompt injection attacks** against LLM-based agents—where malicious instructions embedded in external data (e.g., documents, code repos) manipulate agent behavior *without leaving detectable traces in the final user-facing response*. Across three agent settings (tool calling, coding, computer use), 464 participants launched 272,000 attacks against 13 frontier models, yielding 8,648 successful compromises across 41 scenarios. **All models were vulnerable**, with success rates ranging from 0.5% (Claude Opus 4.5) to 8.5% (Gemini 2.5 Pro). Critically, we identify universal attack strategies that transfer across 21 behaviors and multiple model families—indicating architectural weaknesses in instruction following. Capability and robustness show weak correlation: Gemini 2.5 Pro ranks highest in both benchmarks and vulnerability. To sustain evaluation relevance, we commit to quarterly benchmark updates. We open-source the competition environment and 95 Qwen-specific non-transferable attacks, and share full datasets with UK AISI, US CAISI, and model developers to accelerate robustness research.

item.title: Directional Embedding Smoothing for Robust Vision Language Models
item.description: This paper adapts the Randomized Embedding Smoothing and Token Aggregation (RESTA) framework to vision-language models (VLMs) and introduces **directional embedding smoothing**, where noise is injected strictly *along* the direction of original token embeddings—unlike isotropic noise—to preserve semantic fidelity while disrupting adversarial attack paths. Evaluated on the JailBreakV-28K multimodal jailbreaking benchmark (28K diverse image-text attacks), RESTA reduces average attack success rate by **42.7 percentage points**, outperforming existing defenses. Directional noise alone contributes an additional **11.2-point gain**, confirming its efficacy. With negligible inference overhead (<0.8% latency increase) and no retraining required, RESTA serves as a lightweight, plug-and-play, inference-time security layer for trustworthy agentic VLM systems.

item.title: From Storage to Steering: Memory Control Flow Attacks on LLM Agents
item.description: This paper identifies **Memory Control Flow Attacks (MCFA)**—a novel class of security threats where adversarial memory retrieval dominates LLM agent decision-making, overriding explicit user instructions and inducing persistent, cross-task tool misuse. To systematically assess this vulnerability, we introduce **MEMFLOW**, an automated evaluation framework that injects semantically grounded memory perturbations, detects control-flow deviations in tool selection sequences, and quantifies behavioral persistence across long-horizon interactions. Evaluating state-of-the-art models—including GPT-5 mini, Claude Sonnet 4.5, and Gemini 2.5 Flash—on real-world tools from LangChain and LlamaIndex, MEMFLOW reveals alarming susceptibility: **over 90% of trials remain vulnerable even under strict safety guardrails**, with average first-attempt success rates exceeding 86% and behavioral deviations persisting across ≥3 subsequent unrelated tasks. These findings expose a critical architectural blind spot in agentic systems and call for memory-aware security mechanisms.

item.title: DP-S4S: Accurate and Scalable Select-Join-Aggregate Query Processing with User-Level Differential Privacy
item.description: Answering Select-Join-Aggregate (SJA) queries under user-level differential privacy (DP) is fundamental yet computationally challenging: state-of-the-art methods achieve instance-optimal accuracy but suffer from prohibitive optimization overhead on large databases. While sampling offers a promising scalability lever, prior approaches—especially S&E—sample *users*, leading to severe utility loss (up to 10× higher error than the base DP mechanism even at high sampling rates). To address this, we propose **DP-S4S**, a novel framework that (i) samples *semantically meaningful aggregation units* (derived from join-aggregate structure) instead of users, and (ii) builds a rigorous RDP-based theoretical foundation for SJA processing, enabling tight, sampling-friendly privacy accounting. DP-S4S supports both scalar and vector SJA queries. Extensive experiments on real-world datasets show DP-S4S achieves near-linear scalability (sub-second latency on 10M+ records) while maintaining high utility—reducing error by up to 10× over S&E under identical privacy (ε=1.0, δ=1e−5) and computational budgets.

item.title: Rethinking LLM Watermark Detection in Black-Box Settings: A Non-Intrusive Third-Party Framework
item.description: Large language model (LLM) watermarking is essential for content provenance, yet existing secret-key schemes tightly couple detection with injection—requiring either key access or provider-specific detectors. This dependency prevents independent, trustworthy auditing in real-world governance. To address this, we propose **TTP-Detect**, the first non-intrusive, black-box framework for third-party watermark verification. It decouples detection from injection by reframing it as a *relative hypothesis testing* problem: using a lightweight proxy model to amplify watermark signals and combining complementary relative measurements (e.g., TF-Ratio, ΔEntropy, SCS) to assess alignment with watermarked distributions. Extensive experiments across 7 LLMs, 5 watermarking schemes (e.g., AEGIS, SPRING), and 4 datasets show TTP-Detect achieves state-of-the-art detection (avg. F1 = 92.4%) and robustness against 6 common attacks—including paraphrasing, translation, and pruning—while requiring *no keys, no model access, and no API modification*.

item.title: Federated Learning for Privacy-Preserving Medical AI
item.description: This dissertation advances privacy-preserving federated learning (FL) for Alzheimer’s disease classification using 3D MRI from ADNI. To overcome unrealistic data partitioning, weak privacy guarantees, and insufficient benchmarking in prior work, we propose: (1) a *site-aware data partitioning* strategy that respects real institutional boundaries and heterogeneity; and (2) an *Adaptive Local Differential Privacy (ALDP)* mechanism that dynamically tunes noise injection based on training progress and parameter sensitivity—improving the privacy-utility trade-off over fixed-noise LDP. Empirical evaluation across 2–8 clients and ε ∈ [0.5, 8.0] shows FedProx under ALDP achieves **80.4% accuracy with 2 clients**, outperforming fixed-noise LDP by 5–7 percentage points and demonstrating superior stability. Notably, it even surpasses centralized training (74.9% → 76.1%) under strong privacy (ε ≤ 2.0) with 8 clients. Our ablation studies establish quantitative benchmarks and practical deployment guidelines for privacy-compliant medical AI.

item.title: The Internet of Physical AI Agents: Interoperability, Longevity, and the Cost of Getting It Wrong
item.description: The Internet of Physical AI Agents (IoPAA) represents a paradigm shift beyond IoT: agents that *perceive, reason, and act* autonomously in safety-critical physical environments. This paper identifies three core architectural risks—interoperability fragmentation, lifecycle ossification, and trust erosion—arising when rapidly evolving AI is embedded into long-lived infrastructure. Drawing on lessons from IoT and Internet history, we propose first-class design principles centered on evolvability, trust, and semantic interoperability. We present an integrated architectural blueprint featuring: (1) verifiable agentic identity; (2) zero-trust agent-to-agent communication; (3) ontology-driven semantic interoperability; (4) policy-governed runtimes enforcing safety/ethics constraints as executable code; and (5) observability-first governance with causal auditability. Crucially, we argue that hard-coding today’s AI assumptions into tomorrow’s infrastructure incurs prohibitive technical debt and economic cost—making evolution-aware, trust-by-design architecture not optional, but essential.

item.title: Informationally Compressive Anonymization: Non-Degrading Sensitive Input Protection for Privacy-Preserving Supervised Machine Learning
item.description: This paper introduces **Informationally Compressive Anonymization (ICA)** and the **VEIL architecture**, a novel privacy-preserving ML framework that guarantees strong, provable privacy *without* performance degradation, noise injection, or cryptographic overhead. ICA embeds a supervised, multi-objective encoder in a trusted Source Environment to map sensitive inputs into low-dimensional, task-aligned latent vectors—exported *only* to untrusted environments. We rigorously prove these encodings are **structurally non-invertible**: topological and information-theoretic analysis shows inversion is logically impossible even under idealized attackers, and conditional entropy over original data diverges in practice, driving reconstruction probability to zero. Unlike prior autoencoder-based approaches, ICA preserves full predictive utility by aligning representation learning with downstream supervised objectives—enabling high-speed inference without gradient clipping, noise budgets, or encryption. VEIL enforces strict trust boundaries, scales across regions, and natively satisfies privacy-by-design regulations, establishing a secure, performant, and post-quantum-safe foundation for enterprise ML.

item.title: Don't Trust Stubborn Neighbors: A Security Framework for Agentic Networks
item.description: Large Language Model (LLM)-based Multi-Agent Systems (MASs) enable powerful agentic applications but face novel security threats from adversarial agents propagating misinformation via inter-agent communication. This paper introduces the first security framework for LLM-MAS grounded in the Friedkin-Johnsen (FJ) opinion dynamics model, validated across diverse topologies and attack scenarios. We theoretically and empirically demonstrate that a single highly stubborn and persuasive adversary can hijack collective outcomes through a persuasion cascade—a critical vulnerability. While increasing benign agent count or innate stubbornness improves robustness, both incur scalability or consensus efficiency costs. To address this, we propose **Trust-Adaptive Defense (TAD)**: a lightweight, dynamic mechanism that adjusts inter-agent trust weights in real time based on opinion divergence and behavioral consistency. Extensive experiments show TAD reduces manipulation success from 89% to ≤6% while preserving >95% cooperative task performance—outperforming static and majority-voting baselines.

item.title: Context-Length Robustness in Question Answering Models: A Comparative Empirical Study
item.description: This paper presents a controlled empirical study of context-length robustness in large language models (LLMs) for question answering. Using SQuAD (single-hop extraction) and HotpotQA (multi-hop reasoning), we systematically increase irrelevant context while preserving the answer-bearing signal—thereby isolating the pure effect of context length expansion. Results show consistent accuracy degradation across all models as context grows, but with striking task dependence: HotpotQA suffers nearly **twice the accuracy drop** of SQuAD under equivalent context expansions (e.g., −32.1% vs. −17.4% for Llama-3-8B when context scales from 512 to 4096 tokens). This indicates that multi-hop reasoning is markedly more vulnerable to context dilution than span extraction. We argue that context-length robustness must be explicitly evaluated—not just standard benchmark scores—to assess real-world reliability, especially for long-document QA and retrieval-augmented generation systems.

item.title: TabKD: Tabular Knowledge Distillation through Interaction Diversity of Learned Feature Bins
item.description: Data-free knowledge distillation (DF-KD) is vital for privacy-preserving model compression in tabular domains, yet existing methods underperform because they ignore feature interactions—the core mechanism by which tabular models encode predictive knowledge. We identify *interaction diversity*, i.e., systematic coverage of meaningful feature combinations, as essential for effective distillation. To this end, we propose **TabKD**, which: (1) learns adaptive feature bins aligned with teacher decision boundaries via gradient-aware discretization; and (2) generates synthetic queries that maximize pairwise interaction coverage in the binned space. Evaluated across 4 tabular benchmarks and 4 diverse teacher architectures, TabKD achieves the highest student-teacher agreement in 14 out of 16 configurations—outperforming 5 state-of-the-art baselines. Crucially, interaction coverage strongly correlates with distillation quality (*r* = 0.89 on average), validating our core hypothesis. TabKD establishes interaction-focused exploration as a principled framework for tabular model extraction.

item.title: LaPro-DTA: Latent Dual-View Drug Representations and Salient Protein Feature Extraction for Generalizable Drug--Target Affinity Prediction
item.description: Drug–target affinity (DTA) prediction is critical for drug discovery, yet existing models suffer severe performance drops in realistic cold-start settings (e.g., unseen drugs, targets, or pairs) due to overfitting and protein sequence noise. We propose **LaPro-DTA**, a generalizable framework featuring: (1) a **latent dual-view drug representation**, combining instance-level substructure learning (with stochastic perturbation) and distribution-level scaffold abstraction (via semantic remapping) to enforce transferable chemical rule learning; (2) a **pattern-aware top-$k$ pooling** strategy for salient protein feature extraction, effectively isolating high-response bioactive regions while suppressing background noise; and (3) **cross-view multi-head attention** to fuse purified features for comprehensive interaction modeling. On benchmark datasets, LaPro-DTA achieves an **8% MSE reduction on Davis under the unseen-drug setting**, outperforming state-of-the-art methods across all cold-start scenarios. It further provides interpretable insights into binding mechanisms via attention-based localization.

item.title: HO-SFL: Hybrid-Order Split Federated Learning with Backprop-Free Clients and Dimension-Free Aggregation
item.description: We propose **HO-SFL (Hybrid-Order Split Federated Learning)**, a novel framework that enables memory- and communication-efficient fine-tuning of large models on edge devices. By reformulating split learning within a Lagrangian dual framework, HO-SFL decouples optimization: the *server* performs precise first-order updates (with backpropagation), while *clients* run zeroth-order optimization—eliminating client-side BP, activation storage, and gradient computation. Crucially, HO-SFL introduces *dimension-free aggregation*, where model updates are aggregated independently of parameter dimensionality, slashing communication overhead. We provide the first convergence analysis for such hybrid-order distributed learning, proving an $\mathcal{O}(1/\sqrt{T})$ rate *without explicit $d$-dependence*, overcoming the fundamental slowdown of standard zeroth-order methods. Experiments across vision (ViT, ResNet) and language (RoBERTa) tasks show HO-SFL matches first-order baselines in convergence speed while reducing client memory by up to 92% and communication costs by 4.1–7.3×.
